steps.title: Ik kom uit...
steps.desc: Your first new verb since zijn and hebben. Learn all forms of 'komen' and say where you come from.

steps.note:
Full form: ko-men. The 'o' is long (like 'oh').
You'll hear why this matters for the ik-form.
steps.deepDive:
Open syllable = ends in a vowel, not a consonant.
ko-men: 'ko' ends in vowel → long 'oh'.
Single 'o' is enough — the open syllable does the work.

steps.note:
Short 'o' — just one letter, like English 'cot'.
NOT 'koom'!
steps.deepDive:
ko-men → ik kom.
The open syllable (ko-) became closed (kom).
Closed syllable = ends in a consonant.
Short vowel → single letter: one 'o'.
This is a SHORT-VOWEL verb: the base vowel is short.

steps.note:
In questions: 'Kom jij...?' — the -t drops.
Same rule as 'Heb jij?' and 'Ben jij?'

Same form as jij: 'komt'.
The -t stays in u questions: 'Komt u uit...?'
Same form again: 'komt'. He, she, it, you: all 'komt'.

steps.also: 'zij' also means 'they', but then the verb changes: 'zij komen'
steps.note: 'Zij' = she. Same form as hij: 'komt'.

steps.title: Komen: to come
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note: Two forms to remember: 'kom' for ik, 'komt' for jij, u, hij, zij. Plural = the full form 'komen'.

deepDive.title: The -t drop: it works here too
deepDive.text:
You learned in Unit 1 that jij drops the -t in questions:

Jij hebt een hond. → Heb jij een hond?
Jij bent groot. → Ben jij groot?

Same with komen:

Jij komt uit Nederland. → Kom jij uit Nederland?

This rule works with EVERY verb when jij follows it. You'll see it again and again.

steps.note:
'Wij' = we. Uses the full form: 'komen'.
Short form: 'we komen'.

'Zij' = they here. The verb tells you which:
zij komt = she comes
zij komen = they come

Also called 'Holland' — technically only 2 of 12 provinces.
The 'e' is a quick, lazy 'uh' sound.

'Duits' = German (adjective).
The 'ui' diphthong again — round your lips!

'-rijk' = realm/kingdom.
Frankrijk = realm of the Franks.
Oostenrijk (Austria) = eastern realm!
Your southern neighbour. They speak Dutch too (called 'Vlaams' or Flemish), plus French and German.

steps.hint: Plural: same form as the infinitive

steps.title: Landen en Talen
steps.desc: Your second new verb: spreken (to speak). Plus four languages, so you can say what you speak.

steps.note:
Full form: spre-ken. The 'e' is long (like 'ay').
Single 'e' is enough here.
steps.deepDive:
Open syllable: 'spre' ends in a vowel → long 'ay'.
The open syllable does the work, no need for 'ee'.

steps.note:
Double 'ee' — keeps the long 'ay' sound.
One 'e' alone in 'sprek' would sound short.
steps.deepDive:
spre-ken → ik spreek.
Open syllable (spre-) became closed (spreek).
To keep the LONG vowel → double the letter: 'ee'.
This is a LONG-VOWEL verb.

steps.note:
In questions: 'Spreek jij...?' — the -t drops.
Same rule as every verb.

Goes right before what it modifies.
'Ook' is one of the most common Dutch words.

Same form as jij: 'spreekt'.
The -t stays with u: 'Spreekt u Frans?'
Same form for hij, zij (she), het.
'Zij' here = she. When 'zij' means 'they', the verb changes to the plural form: 'zij spreken'.

steps.title: Spreken: to speak
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note: Two forms: 'spreek' (ik) and 'spreekt' (jij, u, hij, zij). Plural = the full form 'spreken'.

Uses the full form: 'spreken'.
Short form: 'we spreken'.

'Zij' = they here.
Verb tells you: spreekt = she, spreken = they.

Capital letter — derives from a country name.
'Nederlands' (language) vs 'Nederland' (country).
From 'Engeland'. Capital letter, same rule.
From 'Frankrijk' (France).
From 'Duitsland'. That 'ui' diphthong again!

steps.title: 🌍 Multilingual neighbours
steps.text:
The Netherlands and Belgium are famously multilingual:

Most Dutch people speak Engels (English) fluently, often Duits (German) and Frans (French) too.

Belgium has THREE official languages: Nederlands (in Flanders), Frans (in Wallonia), and Duits (in a small eastern region).

Brussel is officially bilingual: Nederlands + Frans.

Saying 'Ik spreek een beetje Nederlands' (I speak a little Dutch) will always get you a warm reaction!

steps.title: Waar woon jij?
steps.desc: Your third new verb: wonen (to live/reside). Plus cities, so you can say where you live.

steps.note:
Full form: wo-nen. The 'o' is long (like 'oh').
Same pattern as ko-men.
steps.deepDive:
Open syllable: 'wo' ends in a vowel → long 'oh'.
Single 'o' is enough — same as ko-men.

steps.note:
Double 'oo' — keeps the long 'oh' sound.
OPPOSITE of 'ik kom' (short 'o')!
steps.deepDive:
wo-nen → ik woon.
Open syllable (wo-) became closed (woon).
To keep the LONG vowel → double: 'oo'.
Opposite of ik kom (short-vowel verb).

steps.note: In questions: 'Woon jij in Brussel?' The -t drops when jij follows, just like every verb.

Same as jij: 'woont'.
The -t stays with u: 'Woont u hier?'
Same form for hij, zij (she), het.
'Zij' = she. For 'they': 'zij wonen'.

steps.title: Wonen: to live
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note: Two forms: 'woon' (ik) and 'woont' (jij, u, hij, zij). Plural = the full form 'wonen'.

steps.example: Wij wonen in een klein dorp.
steps.exampleEn: We live in a small village.
steps.note:
Uses the full form: 'wonen'.
Short form: 'we wonen'.

'Zij' = they here.
woont = she, wonen = they.

Largest city, but government sits in Den Haag.
Cultural and business capital.

Capital of Belgium AND the EU.
Bilingual city: Dutch and French.

steps.title: 🏠 'In' vs 'uit'
steps.text:
Two little words, big difference:

Ik woon IN Amsterdam. = I live in Amsterdam.
Ik kom UIT Nederland. = I come from the Netherlands.

'In' = location (where you are now)
'Uit' = origin (where you're from)

You can combine them:
Ik kom uit België en ik woon in Amsterdam.
= I come from Belgium and I live in Amsterdam.

steps.hint: Question with jij: what happens to the -t?

steps.title: Zijn of Hebben?
steps.desc: You already know all forms of zijn and hebben. Now master CHOOSING the right one, mixed with your new verbs.
goals: Pick the right verb: zijn or hebben

steps.note:
Zijn = what something IS (identity, state).
Hebben = what someone HAS (possession).
Think: am/is/are vs have/has.
steps.example: Hij IS leraar. Hij HEEFT een hond.
steps.exampleEn: He IS a teacher. He HAS a dog.

steps.title: ⚠️ Tricky cases
steps.text:
Age uses ZIJN (not hebben!):
Ik ben 25 jaar oud. = I am 25 years old.

Hunger and thirst use HEBBEN:
Ik heb honger. = I am hungry. (lit: I have hunger)
Ik heb dorst. = I am thirsty. (lit: I have thirst)

French and Spanish speakers: Dutch uses 'hebben' for hunger/thirst like your languages, but 'zijn' for age unlike yours!

steps.hint: A feeling you possess, not something you are

steps.title: Alles over Jou
steps.desc: Put it all together: six key questions about identity. After this lesson, you can introduce yourself completely in Dutch.

steps.note:
Five questions that cover your full identity.
See the tip below for the complete list.
steps.example: Hoe heet je? Ik heet Anna.
steps.exampleEn: What's your name? My name is Anna.

steps.note:
No article before professions with zijn:
'Ik ben leraar' NOT 'Ik ben een leraar'.

steps.exampleEn: She is a student in Amsterdam.
steps.note: A cognate — looks and sounds almost identical.

steps.example: Hoe oud ben je? Ik ben 25.
steps.exampleEn: How old are you? I am 25.
steps.note:
Literally: 'how old are you?'
The answer uses zijn + number + jaar:
'Ik ben vijfentwintig jaar (oud).' — 'oud' is optional.

'Jaar' = year. No plural needed!
Dutch says 'dertig jaar', NOT 'dertig jaren'.
Just like English: 'I am 30', not 'I am 30 years'.

steps.hint: What do you do with a language?

steps.s: Ik {1} uit Frankrijk en ik {2} Frans.
Hij {1} leraar en hij {2} in Brussel.

steps.title: 🙋 You can now introduce yourself completely
steps.text:
Name: Ik heet...
Origin: Ik kom uit...
Language: Ik spreek...
Home: Ik woon in...
Age: Ik ben ... jaar.
Profession: Ik ben...

That's six questions answered with five verbs. You've come a long way since 'Hallo'!

steps.title: More de & het Nouns
steps.desc: You learned de and het in Unit 1. Now: 8 essential nouns to build your vocabulary.
goals: Spot cognates with English and German

steps.note: One of the most common de-words.
'Vrouw' also means 'wife' — context tells you.

steps.example: Het kind is drie jaar oud.
steps.exampleEn: The child is three years old.
steps.note: False friend: NOT English 'kind' (= aardig).
Plural: steden (irregular).

steps.exampleEn: The Netherlands is a small country.
steps.note: Means both 'country' and 'land' (ground).
No close English cognate — just memorize it.
'ui' = Dutch diphthong. Like 'ow' in 'house'.

steps.example: De school is in de stad.
steps.exampleEn: The school is in the city.
steps.note:
Almost identical to English.
'sch' = 'skh', not 'sh'.

steps.title: 🔵🟡 De or Het?
steps.text:
About 75% of Dutch nouns are de-words. Het-words include:

• Words ending in -je (broodje, meisje, kopje)
• Many two-syllable words starting with be-, ge-, ver-
• And many others you simply learn by exposure

The best strategy: always learn the article WITH the noun. Say 'het huis', never just 'huis'.

deepDive.title: De and het: does it matter?
deepDive.text:
Yes. Later, de and het affect:

• Which demonstrative you use (dit/dat vs deze/die)
• Which relative pronoun you use (dat vs die)
• Adjective endings in some cases

Getting articles wrong won't stop people from understanding you, but it marks you as a beginner. Building the instinct now saves pain later.

steps.hint: Think about the teach card. Child = ?

steps.title: Er is... Er zijn...
steps.desc: How do you say 'there is' and 'there are' in Dutch? Two tiny words change everything.
goals: Use er is for singular things
Use er zijn for plural things
Negate with er is geen and er zijn geen

steps.example: Er is een man in het huis.
steps.exampleEn: There is a man in the house.
steps.note:
Used with ONE thing.
'Er' = Dutch sentence starter.

steps.example: Er zijn twee kinderen in de school.
steps.exampleEn: There are two children in the school.
steps.note:
Used with MORE THAN ONE.
Same 'zijn' you know from 'they are'.

steps.title: 📍 Er = there (sort of)
steps.text:
'Er' doesn't translate perfectly to English.

English can skip 'there': 'A man is in the house.'
Dutch always needs 'er': 'Er is een man in het huis.'

Think of 'er' as a sentence starter that Dutch requires when talking about what exists somewhere.

deepDive.title: Why does Dutch need 'er'?
deepDive.text:
Dutch doesn't like starting a sentence with an unknown subject.

Instead of just saying 'Een man is in het huis', Dutch prefers 'Er is een man in het huis.'

This 'er' signals: I'm about to tell you something exists.

You'll see 'er' again in later units with other uses, but for now: er is = there is, er zijn = there are.

steps.note: 'Geen' negates nouns, just like you learned in Unit 1. Er is + geen = there is no.

steps.example: Er zijn geen kinderen in de school.
steps.exampleEn: There are no children in the school.
steps.note: Plural version. 'Geen' works for both.

steps.q: Er ___ een school in de stad.
Er ___ drie mannen in het huis.

steps.s: Er ___ vijf huizen in de stad.

steps.q: How do you say 'There is no house'?
steps.hint: One house = singular, negate with geen

steps.s: Er ___ twee vrouwen en een man.
steps.hint: Two women + one man = plural total

steps.s: Er {1} een school en er {2} drie huizen.

steps.title: Dit & Dat, Deze & Die
steps.desc: This, that, these, those. Dutch has a system that connects to de and het. Two uses, one clear pattern.
goals: Use deze/die with de-words and plurals
Point at things: always Dit is... / Dat is...

steps.note:
Points at something nearby.
'dit huis hier' = this house here.

Points at something farther away.
'die school daar' = that school there.

For het-words nearby.
'Dit huis hier' = this house here.

For het-words farther away.
'Dat huis daar' = that house there.

steps.example: Deze man hier is een leraar.
steps.exampleEn: This man here is a teacher.
steps.note:
For de-words + plurals nearby.
'Deze huizen hier' = these houses here.

For de-words + plurals farther away.
'Die kinderen daar' = those children there.
steps.also: Later: 'die' also means 'who/that' in relative clauses.

steps.title: 👉 Two Uses of Demonstratives
steps.text:
BEFORE A NOUN:
het-words nearby: dit huis hier
het-words far: dat huis daar
de-words nearby: deze man hier
de-words far: die man daar
Plurals: same as de-words (deze/die)

POINTING AT SOMETHING:
Dit is het huis. Dat is de school.
Always dit/dat when pointing at things.
Never 'deze is de school' or 'die is het huis'.

deepDive.title: Why always dit/dat for pointing?
deepDive.text:
When you point at a thing and say 'this is...' or 'that is...', Dutch always uses dit or dat.

It does not matter if the noun is de or het:
Dit is de man. (pointing nearby)
Dat is de school. (pointing far)
Dit is het kind. (pointing nearby)
Dat is het huis. (pointing far)

Think of 'Dit is...' and 'Dat is...' as fixed phrases for pointing.

'Deze is...' and 'Die is...' are only used when choosing between people, not things.

steps.q: How do you say 'That man there is not here'?
opts: Die man daar is niet hier
Deze man daar is niet hier
Dat man daar is niet hier
Dit man daar is niet hier
steps.ans: Die man daar is niet hier

steps.hint: Pointing at something nearby: always dit
Pointing at something far: always dat

steps.title: ⚠️ A Common Mistake
steps.text:
Dit is de school. (correct)
Deze is de school. (wrong)

When pointing at things, always use dit or dat.
Never deze or die.

Deze and die go BEFORE a noun:
Deze school hier is groot. (correct)

But when pointing: Dit is... / Dat is... Always.

deepDive.title: When CAN you use deze/die alone?
deepDive.text:
Only when choosing between people:
'Welke man? Deze.' = Which man? This one.

At this stage, just remember the rule:
Pointing at things = dit/dat.
Before a noun = check de/het + hier/daar.

steps.s: {1} huis hier is groot en {2} stad daar is mooi.

steps.title: Mini-Dialogue: Meeting Someone
steps.desc: Time to put it all together. A real Dutch conversation between two people meeting for the first time.
goals: Fill in missing parts of a conversation

steps.title: 🤝 The Conversation
steps.text:
A: Hallo! Hoe heet je?
B: Ik heet Sophie. En jij?

A: Ik heet Marco. Aangenaam!
B: Aangenaam! Waar kom je vandaan?

A: Ik kom uit Duitsland. En jij?
B: Ik kom uit België. Ik woon in Brussel.

A: Spreek je ook Frans?
B: Ja, ik spreek Frans en Nederlands.

deepDive.title: Natural conversation structure
deepDive.text:
Notice the pattern:
1. Greeting (Hallo!)
2. Names (Hoe heet je?)
3. Origin (Waar kom je vandaan?)
4. Bounce back (En jij?)
5. Details (language, residence)

This is how Dutch people actually start conversations. The 'en jij?' bounce-back is key. It keeps the conversation flowing.

steps.q: What does 'Waar kom je vandaan?' mean?

steps.s: Ik kom uit ___. Ik woon in Brussel.
steps.hint: Sophie lives in Brussels, so she's from...
Ja, ik spreek Frans en Nederlands.

steps.s: Ik {1} in Brussel en ik {2} Frans.
Ik {1} in Amsterdam en ik {2} een beetje Nederlands.

steps.title: 🎉 Your first real Dutch conversation!
steps.text:
You can now introduce yourself, ask about someone's origin, and talk about languages.

Name: Ik heet...
Origin: Ik kom uit...
Residence: Ik woon in...
Language: Ik spreek...

That's real A1 progress. You've come a long way since 'Hallo'!

steps.title: Unit 2 Review
steps.desc: Everything from Unit 2, plus Unit 1 mixed in. No new words. Just proof you've got this.
goals: Review all 5 verbs and their forms
Test de/het articles, er is/zijn, dit/dat/deze/die

steps.s: Er ___ drie scholen in de stad.
Er {1} een man en er {2} twee vrouwen.
